description: Figure 17 presents experimental and simulated microstructure evolutions in low-alloy steel during hot compression at 950 °C under constant and transiently increasing strain rates. Panels (a) and (b) show experimental EBSD reconstructed inverse pole figure maps and corresponding GOS maps. At 0.9 strain, constant 0.1 s⁻¹ yields an average grain size of 28.89±3.09 μm with 95.3% DRX fraction, whereas transiently increasing from 0.01 to 0.1 s⁻¹ at ε_I=0.2 and 0.4 produces coarser grains (31.93±2.03 μm and 37.16±2.26 μm, respectively) with similarly high DRX fractions (~94–96%), indicating that delayed strain-rate transitions leave more residual first-stage grains. Panel (b) further compares microstructures at intermediate strains, showing grain refinement from 65.33 μm (0.01 s⁻¹, 0.4 strain) toward 50.53 μm (0.1 s⁻¹, 0.5 strain). Panels (c), (d), and (e) display 3D cellular automaton simulations: (c) under constant 0.1 s⁻¹, grain size evolves from 119.27 μm at 0.3 strain (13.0% DRX) to 31.07 μm at 0.9 strain (98.7% DRX); (d) for a strain-rate jump at ε_I=0.2, DRX progresses slowly to 98.2% by 0.9 strain (33.77 μm); and (e) for a jump at ε_I=0.4, 76.9% DRX at 0.4 strain rises to 97.9% at 0.9 strain (37.12 μm), with white, green, and red grains denoting matrix, first-stage, and second-stage grains, respectively. The results collectively validate that the inhomogeneous-dislocation-density 3D CA model captures multi-stage DRX evolution and that transient strain-rate increases promote heterogeneous grain structures with larger final average grain sizes than constant high strain-rate conditions.
